subtitle: Agile Coach · Jira AI & Copilot · AI-Assisted Delivery
hero_title:
Not a Jira user.
A Jira AI practitioner.
hero_sub: Most Scrum Masters have Jira on their resume. Fewer have embedded Jira AI and Microsoft Copilot into a live enterprise delivery workflow and measured a 30% overhead reduction from it. That is the distinction your posting is looking for. That is this resume.

the_case.need: Hands-on Jira AI experience for backlog refinement, forecasting, and reporting
the_case.answer: Embedded Jira AI at Crocs into backlog refinement, sprint summaries, and stakeholder reporting — reducing administrative overhead roughly 30% while improving output quality and decision traceability.
the_case.detail: The specific outcome at Crocs: Jira AI automated the weekly release readiness report, sprint outcome summaries, and backlog hygiene checks. I reviewed the output; stakeholders received it. The quality went up because I had time to review instead of generate. The 30% overhead reduction was not from one automation — it was from systematically identifying and replacing every recurring low-value task with AI-assisted output and human review. That is the model the role describes.

the_case.need: Leverage Copilot for sprint summaries, insights, and communication efficiency
the_case.answer: Used Microsoft Copilot at Crocs for stakeholder communication drafts, meeting documentation, and decision logs — directly parallel to the Copilot use this role describes.
the_case.detail: Copilot at Crocs handled async communication drafts to Engineering and Business stakeholders, structured meeting recaps, and decision documentation. The value was not the tool — it was the freed capacity. Every hour not spent formatting a status update was an hour spent on delivery risk, coaching, or cross-team alignment. That is what 'communication efficiency' means in practice.

the_case.need: 5+ years Scrum Master, software/product development teams, Agile environments
the_case.answer: 10+ years — Crocs, Eptura (~10 distributed teams), A&E (~10 concurrent SVOD workstreams), Understood.org, Stealth AI, DigitasLBI, Paperless Post, Underline.
the_case.detail: The five-year ask is exceeded by double. Each engagement has a different scale, domain, and team composition — and the consistent outcomes are: ceremonies running cleanly, POs with execution-ready backlogs, blockers surfacing before they stall a sprint, and delivery metrics that actually inform decisions.

the_case.need: Coach teams, Product Owners, and stakeholders on Agile best practices
the_case.answer: Coached POs at Eptura (~10 teams), A&E (~10 concurrent workstreams), and Paperless Post on backlog readiness, story quality, and acceptance-criteria discipline. Coached stakeholders at Crocs, Understood.org, and Eptura on delivery cadence and Agile operating norms.
the_case.detail: Coaching in three directions simultaneously is a different skill than coaching one direction. At Eptura I was coaching POs on story readiness in the morning and presenting delivery health to senior leadership in the afternoon. At Crocs I was coaching Engineering on Definition of Done discipline while translating compliance requirements into engineering tasks for the Business team. The communication adjusts to the audience; the clarity does not.

the_case.need: DevOps practices and CI/CD pipelines — aligned with Agile delivery
the_case.answer: Owned full CI/CD pipelines via GitHub Actions for six production applications this year. Coordinated Crocs build→test→UAT→cutover lifecycle. Delivered within Azure/Kubernetes environments at Eptura and Understood.org.
the_case.detail: CI/CD familiarity for a Scrum Master means: knowing where in the pipeline a release is at any given moment, understanding what a failed build means for the sprint commitment, and coordinating the ceremony cadence around deployment windows rather than against them. I can read a pipeline, ask the right questions, and hold a substantive conversation with a DevOps engineer about what is actually blocking the release.

the_case.need: Track and analyze Agile delivery metrics — velocity, cycle time, predictability
the_case.answer: Tracked velocity, cycle time, burndown, and predictability as operational instruments at every major engagement. Built Jira and ADO delivery dashboards at Eptura for leadership reporting across ~10 teams. Drove 35%+ efficiency gain and 20% carryover reduction from data-driven coaching interventions.
the_case.detail: Velocity is a planning input, not a performance score. Cycle time tells you where work is actually stalling. Predictability tells you whether the team's commitments are trustworthy. These three metrics together give you the actual picture of delivery health. At Eptura I used them to identify systemic bottlenecks across ~10 teams and drive targeted coaching interventions. The 35% efficiency gain was measured before it was claimed.

the_stack.headline: The AI-augmented Agile toolkit.
the_stack.subhead: Not aspirational — operational. Used in production delivery, not in demos.

nodes.label: Jira AI
nodes.desc: Backlog refinement assistance, sprint summary generation, forecasting, and reporting automation
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs into weekly release governance workflows — ~30% overhead reduction. The output got sharper because I had time to review instead of generate.

nodes.label: Microsoft Copilot
nodes.desc: Sprint summaries, stakeholder communication drafts, meeting documentation, and decision logging
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs for async stakeholder communication and structured meeting recaps. Frees SM capacity from administrative output to actual coaching and delivery risk management.

nodes.label: Jira (admin-level)
nodes.desc: Workflows, boards, dashboards, velocity and burndown reporting, sprint health, portfolio visibility
nodes.pm_role: Built from scratch at A&E (~10 teams). Standardized across Eptura PMO. Configured SOD compliance controls at Crocs. Admin-level fluency — not just daily use.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions / CI/CD
nodes.desc: CI/CD pipeline management across six production applications; release lifecycle coordination
nodes.pm_role: Owned CI/CD pipeline for six production apps via GitHub Actions. Coordinated build→test→UAT→cutover lifecycle at Crocs. Can engage substantively with DevOps on pipeline blockers.

nodes.label: Azure DevOps / Confluence
nodes.desc: ADO configuration and board management; Confluence for team documentation and Agile standards
nodes.pm_role: ADO configuration owner at Eptura PMO. Confluence documentation standardized across teams. Fluent switching between Jira and ADO depending on team environment.

when_things_break.scenario: A team is using Jira AI outputs without reviewing them — a sprint summary with an error reaches a stakeholder
response: Acknowledge the error to the stakeholder directly and immediately — do not let it sit
Correct the summary with verified data and re-send with a clear note on what changed
Identify where the human review step failed: was the output skipped, rushed, or not understood?
Add an explicit AI output review checkpoint to the sprint reporting workflow
Coach the team: AI generates, humans validate — this is the standard, not the exception

when_things_break.scenario: Velocity is trending down for three sprints and the team insists everything is fine
response: Pull cycle time and throughput data alongside velocity — find what the team is not seeing
Bring the data to the retrospective with a specific question: what is taking longer than it used to?
Separate the noise (sprint size variance) from the signal (genuine throughput decline)
Facilitate a structured root cause conversation — not a blame session, a diagnosis
Introduce one targeted intervention, measure the effect, and report the result to the team

when_things_break.scenario: A Product Owner is writing stories so vague the team cannot estimate or build against them
response: Name the pattern in private first — not at sprint planning in front of the team
Coach the PO on the INVEST model and what 'ready' actually means for this team
Run a story-writing workshop using real examples from the backlog — not theory
Introduce a Definition of Ready checklist the team owns, not the SM
Give the PO a safe sprint to practice — protect the relationship while the skill builds

how_i_run_a_room: Every ceremony has a stated outcome — if it doesn't, it doesn't happen
Jira AI and Copilot handle the administrative output — human judgment owns every output before it ships
Blockers surface at standup with a proposed path forward — not at retrospective as a postmortem
Velocity is a planning input; cycle time and predictability tell the real delivery story
POs get coaching before the planning meeting, not during it — readiness is built in grooming
Retrospective action items become the first agenda item for the next retro — not archived notes
DevOps alignment happens before sprint planning, not after the sprint ends

points: Embedded Jira AI and Microsoft Copilot into sprint reporting and stakeholder communication
~30% administrative overhead reduction — documented, not estimated
SOX-compliant release governance with Go/No-Go cadence and CAB-style change approval
On-time go-live with clean post-launch stabilization

track_record.role: Agile Coach & Senior Scrum Master — Enterprise PMO
points: ~10 distributed teams (India and California) — ceremonies, coaching, and Scrum-of-Scrums
35%+ delivery efficiency gain; 20% sprint carryover reduction — data-driven interventions
Coached POs on backlog readiness; mentored junior Scrum Masters org-wide
Standardized Jira and ADO across the PMO; built leadership delivery dashboards

track_record.role: Scrum Master & Agile Coach — Multi-Platform Delivery
points: ~10 concurrent SVOD workstreams — Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, Roku — simultaneously
Built and administered Jira from scratch; two-year cultural transformation
Shipped all platforms against externally committed public launch windows
Coached POs on acceptance-criteria-complete stories sized to a single sprint

track_record.role: Scrum Master — AI Data & Automation Workstreams
points: SM for AI automation and data pipeline teams — Scrum and Kanban delivery
Diagnosed pipeline bottlenecks; ~30% throughput improvement through workflow re-sequencing
Maintained velocity, cycle time, and burndown metrics; escalated impediments proactively

before: Jira AI outputs go out unreviewed — stakeholders receive errors nobody caught
Velocity is reported but nobody acts on it — it is a metric, not a management tool
POs write stories too late and too vague — sprint planning becomes a clarification session
Copilot is available but nobody is using it systematically — recurring tasks still take hours
after: AI output goes through human review before it ships — quality improves, errors surface before stakeholders
Velocity, cycle time, and predictability drive coaching decisions — the team sees what the data shows
Stories arrive at planning ready to build — Definition of Ready is owned by the team, not the SM
Copilot handles recurring documentation and communication — SM time goes to coaching and delivery risk

the_ramp.deliverable: Meet the teams, POs, and stakeholders. Audit current Jira setup, ceremony cadence, and delivery metrics. Understand which AI tools are available and how they are currently being used — or not used.
Facilitate first full ceremony cycle. Identify the top 3 gaps in AI tool adoption and backlog readiness. Propose the first Jira AI workflow improvement with a measurable baseline.
First Jira AI automation live and reviewed. Copilot embedded in at least one recurring SM workflow. Delivery metrics dashboard publishing reliably for team and leadership visibility.

Teams running ceremonies independently with improved delivery predictability. AI tools embedded in at least three recurring delivery workflows. First retrospective improvement documented and implemented.
